model_narrative: A village in the Tabora region must supply its own fuelwood and curing wood over a thirty-year horizon, choosing each year how much natural forest to cut from each of nine rings around the village, how much timber to plant and manage, and how many hectares of maize and tobacco to grow. Cutting the natural forest gets harder over time because the closer rings are exhausted and the village must travel farther, while planted timber takes years to mature but provides a sustainable supply. The village also tracks the labor it has available each month, the maize it needs to feed itself, and the extra maize yield it gets on land grown right after tobacco. Each year produces crop revenue and incurs planting, cutting, wage, and transport costs, and the goal is to maximize the total discounted net income over the whole horizon.

sets.name: t
sets.doc: the planning years in chronological order, indexed by the integers 1 through 30; the first year is 1 and each later year follows the one before it

sets.name: a
sets.doc: the age of planted timber in years, from 1 up to 24; timber planted in one year reaches age a exactly a years later

sets.name: m
sets.doc: the twelve calendar months over which labor is supplied and used

sets.name: mc
sets.doc: the cutting months, the subset of months during which wood is cut from the forest

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the nine concentric annuli (rings) of natural forest around the village; ring 1 is closest and ring 9 is farthest, so cutting a farther ring costs more travel time

sets.name: c
sets.doc: the two annual crops the village can grow, maize and tobacco

params.name: yw
params.doc: yield of the existing natural forest, in cubic metres of wood per hectare cut

params.name: yv
params.doc: yield of planted timber at each tree age a, in cubic metres per hectare; a hectare planted t-a years ago and managed now yields yv[a]

params.name: wrc
params.doc: wood needed to cure one hectare of tobacco, in cubic metres per hectare

params.name: dwr
params.doc: domestic wood requirement per family per year, in cubic metres per family

params.name: nfam
params.doc: number of families living in the village

params.name: sfam
params.doc: size of a family in adult-equivalent workers

params.name: wdm
params.doc: working days available per worker per month

params.name: ld
params.doc: labor requirement table in man-days, by activity and month; activities include 'timber-1' and 'timber-2' (managing planted timber in its first and second year), the crop names, and 'other' (non-farm labor that reduces the labor pool); entries default to zero where not listed

params.name: labwc
params.doc: labor to cut the natural forest, in man-days per cubic metre

params.name: labvc
params.doc: labor to cut planted timber, in man-days per cubic metre

params.name: ws
params.doc: walking speed when travelling to the forest, in kilometres per hour

params.name: whd
params.doc: work hours available per day

params.name: sr
params.doc: starting radius of the innermost annulus, in kilometres

params.name: width
params.doc: radial width of each annulus, in kilometres; the average distance to ring i is sr - width/2 + width*i and its area is 100*pi*(that distance)*width hectares

params.name: tmd
params.doc: crop data table by crop, with fields 'input-cost' (tsh per hectare), 'yield' (kg per hectare), and 'price' (tsh per kg); crop revenue per hectare is yield times price, crop cost per hectare is the input-cost

params.name: fdmaize
params.doc: domestic maize demand per family per year, in kilograms

params.name: matr
params.doc: the fractional improvement in maize yield on land grown with maize in the year right after tobacco; such land yields matr times the normal maize output

params.name: tob
params.doc: steady-state tobacco area, in hectares; also the upper bound on maize-after-tobacco area available in the first year

params.name: tc
params.doc: cost of planting timber, in tsh per hectare

params.name: resw
params.doc: reservation wage, in tsh per hour, charged for labor spent cutting wood

params.name: tr
params.doc: transport cost of wood, in tsh per cubic metre per kilometre

params.name: rho
params.doc: annual discount rate; the discount factor applied to year t is (1+rho)**(-t)

vars.name: w
vars.doc: hectares of natural forest cut in ring i during year t

vars.name: v
vars.doc: hectares of planted timber managed (planted) in year t; timber planted in year t-a is available at age a in year t

vars.name: x
vars.doc: hectares of crop c grown in year t

vars.name: mat
vars.doc: hectares of maize grown after tobacco in year t, which enjoys the improved post-tobacco yield

vars.name: lc
vars.doc: man-days of labor devoted to wood cutting in year t and month m; only meaningful in cutting months

vars.name: rev
vars.doc: total crop revenue in year t, in thousands of tsh

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: total cost in year t, in thousands of tsh

vars.name: income
vars.doc: total discounted net income over the whole 30-year horizon, in thousands of tsh

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: Each year the wood the village obtains must cover the wood it needs. For every year, the wood cut from the natural forest plus the wood yielded by the planted timber maturing that year must be at least the wood required to cure that year's tobacco plus the domestic wood the families need.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: The natural forest in each ring is finite. For every ring, the total area cut from that ring over all the years must not exceed the area of that ring.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: The village cannot use more labor in a month than it has. For every year and every month, the labor spent managing newly planted and second-year timber, the labor spent on the crops, and, in cutting months, the labor spent cutting wood together must not exceed the labor the village has available that month.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: Wood cutting requires labor in the cutting months. For every year, the labor devoted to wood cutting across the cutting months must equal the labor needed to cut the natural forest harvested that year plus the labor needed to cut the planted timber maturing that year.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: The village must feed itself. For every year, the maize produced, counting both ordinary maize land and the extra yield from maize grown right after tobacco, must be at least the village's domestic maize demand.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: Timber planted too close to the end of the horizon would not mature in time to be useful, so it is limited. For every year, the timber that would only reach harvestable age after the horizon ends must yield no more than the village's total domestic and curing wood requirement.

constraints.name: matd1
constraints.intent: Maize can only get the post-tobacco boost on land that actually grew tobacco the year before. For every year after the first, the area of maize-after-tobacco must not exceed the tobacco area grown in the previous year.

constraints.name: matd2
constraints.intent: Maize-after-tobacco is still maize land. For every year, the area of maize-after-tobacco must not exceed the maize area grown that year.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: Revenue must reflect what the crops earn. For every year, the revenue equals the revenue from each crop's area plus the extra revenue from the improved yield on maize grown after tobacco, expressed in thousands.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: Cost must reflect what the village spends. For every year, the cost equals crop input costs, timber planting cost, the wage paid for wood-cutting labor in the cutting months, and the transport cost of hauling cut natural-forest wood, all expressed in thousands.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: Income must reflect discounted net returns. The total discounted income equals, summed over all years and discounted to the present, each year's revenue minus its cost plus the discounted residual value of the timber still standing.

query: We're toying with a cap on how much tobacco the village grows over the whole horizon — no more than 6000 hectares of tobacco summed across all 30 years. What does the optimizer return for discounted income under that ceiling?
expected_pyomo: model.tobacco_total_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[t, 'tobacco'] for t in model.t) <= 6000)

query: Suppose we limit how hard the village leans on the natural forest in the opening year — hold total cutting of existing forest in year one to at most 300 hectares. Where does discounted income come out?
expected_pyomo: model.forest_cut_y01_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.w[1, i] for i in model.i) <= 300)

query: There's a proposal on the table to keep the maize-after-tobacco rotation in proportion to the village's straight maize cropping — the total rotation area over the horizon shouldn't run past three-fifths of the total land in direct maize. What does the optimizer return for discounted income under that rule?
expected_pyomo: model.rotation_share_cap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.mat[t] for t in model.t) <= 0.6 * sum(model.x[t, 'maize'] for t in model.t))

t)
def
constraints.intent: The natural forest in each ring is finite. For every ring, the total area cut from that ring over all the years must not exceed the area of that ring.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: The village cannot use more labor in a month than it has. For every year and every month, the labor spent managing newly planted and second-year timber, the labor spent on the crops, and, in cutting months, the labor spent cutting wood together must not exceed the labor the village has available that month.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: Wood cutting requires labor in the cutting months. For every year, the labor devoted to wood cutting across the cutting months must equal the labor needed to cut the natural forest harvested that year plus the labor needed to cut the planted timber maturing that year.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: The village must feed itself. For every year, the maize produced, counting both ordinary maize land and the extra yield from maize grown right after tobacco, must be at least the village's domestic maize demand.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: Timber planted too close to the end of the horizon would not mature in time to be useful, so it is limited. For every year, the timber that would only reach harvestable age after the horizon ends must yield no more than the village's total domestic and curing wood requirement.

constraints.name: matd1
constraints.intent: Maize can only get the post-tobacco boost on land that actually grew tobacco the year before. For every year after the first, the area of maize-after-tobacco must not exceed the tobacco area grown in the previous year.

constraints.name: matd2
constraints.intent: Maize-after-tobacco is still maize land. For every year, the area of maize-after-tobacco must not exceed the maize area grown that year.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: Revenue must reflect what the crops earn. For every year, the revenue equals the revenue from each crop's area plus the extra revenue from the improved yield on maize grown after tobacco, expressed in thousands.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: Cost must reflect what the village spends. For every year, the cost equals crop input costs, timber planting cost, the wage paid for wood-cutting labor in the cutting months, and the transport cost of hauling cut natural-forest wood, all expressed in thousands.

constraints.name:
t)
def
constraints.intent: Income must reflect discounted net returns. The total discounted income equals, summed over all years and discounted to the present, each year's revenue minus its cost plus the discounted residual value of the timber still standing.

query: The plan runs tobacco flat out at well over two hundred hectares every single year, which feels like a lot of land tied to one cash crop year after year. Shouldn't we hold each year's tobacco to no more than 210 hectares right across the thirty-year horizon? What's the model seeing that makes it want to keep tobacco pinned that high every year?

model_narrative: A village in the Tabora region must supply its own fuelwood and curing wood over a thirty-year horizon, choosing each year how much natural forest to cut from each of nine rings around the village, how much timber to plant and manage, and how many hectares of maize and tobacco to grow. Cutting the natural forest gets harder over time because the closer rings are exhausted and the village must travel farther, while planted timber takes years to mature but provides a sustainable supply. The village also tracks the labor it has available each month, the maize it needs to feed itself, and the extra maize yield it gets on land grown right after tobacco. Each year produces crop revenue and incurs planting, cutting, wage, and transport costs, and the goal is to maximize the total discounted net income over the whole horizon.

sets.name: t
sets.doc: the planning years in chronological order, indexed by the integers 1 through 30; the first year is 1 and each later year follows the one before it

sets.name: a
sets.doc: the age of planted timber in years, from 1 up to 24; timber planted in one year reaches age a exactly a years later

sets.name: m
sets.doc: the twelve calendar months over which labor is supplied and used

sets.name: mc
sets.doc: the cutting months, the subset of months during which wood is cut from the forest

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the nine concentric annuli (rings) of natural forest around the village; ring 1 is closest and ring 9 is farthest, so cutting a farther ring costs more travel time

sets.name: c
sets.doc: the two annual crops the village can grow, maize and tobacco

params.name: yw
params.doc: yield of the existing natural forest, in cubic metres of wood per hectare cut

params.name: yv
params.doc: yield of planted timber at each tree age a, in cubic metres per hectare; a hectare planted t-a years ago and managed now yields yv[a]

params.name: wrc
params.doc: wood needed to cure one hectare of tobacco, in cubic metres per hectare

params.name: dwr
params.doc: domestic wood requirement per family per year, in cubic metres per family

params.name: nfam
params.doc: number of families living in the village

params.name: sfam
params.doc: size of a family in adult-equivalent workers

params.name: wdm
params.doc: working days available per worker per month

params.name: ld
params.doc: labor requirement table in man-days, by activity and month; activities include 'timber-1' and 'timber-2' (managing planted timber in its first and second year), the crop names, and 'other' (non-farm labor that reduces the labor pool); entries default to zero where not listed

params.name: labwc
params.doc: labor to cut the natural forest, in man-days per cubic metre

params.name: labvc
params.doc: labor to cut planted timber, in man-days per cubic metre

params.name: ws
params.doc: walking speed when travelling to the forest, in kilometres per hour

params.name: whd
params.doc: work hours available per day

params.name: sr
params.doc: starting radius of the innermost annulus, in kilometres

params.name: width
params.doc: radial width of each annulus, in kilometres; the average distance to ring i is sr - width/2 + width*i and its area is 100*pi*(that distance)*width hectares

params.name: tmd
params.doc: crop data table by crop, with fields 'input-cost' (tsh per hectare), 'yield' (kg per hectare), and 'price' (tsh per kg); crop revenue per hectare is yield times price, crop cost per hectare is the input-cost

params.name: fdmaize
params.doc: domestic maize demand per family per year, in kilograms

params.name: matr
params.doc: the fractional improvement in maize yield on land grown with maize in the year right after tobacco; such land yields matr times the normal maize output

params.name: tob
params.doc: steady-state tobacco area, in hectares; also the upper bound on maize-after-tobacco area available in the first year

params.name: tc
params.doc: cost of planting timber, in tsh per hectare

params.name: resw
params.doc: reservation wage, in tsh per hour, charged for labor spent cutting wood

params.name: tr
params.doc: transport cost of wood, in tsh per cubic metre per kilometre

params.name: rho
params.doc: annual discount rate; the discount factor applied to year t is (1+rho)**(-t)

vars.name: w
vars.doc: hectares of natural forest cut in ring i during year t

vars.name: v
vars.doc: hectares of planted timber managed (planted) in year t; timber planted in year t-a is available at age a in year t

vars.name: x
vars.doc: hectares of crop c grown in year t

vars.name: mat
vars.doc: hectares of maize grown after tobacco in year t, which enjoys the improved post-tobacco yield

vars.name: lc
vars.doc: man-days of labor devoted to wood cutting in year t and month m; only meaningful in cutting months

vars.name: rev
vars.doc: total crop revenue in year t, in thousands of tsh

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: total cost in year t, in thousands of tsh

vars.name: income
vars.doc: total discounted net income over the whole 30-year horizon, in thousands of tsh

query: Cutting nearly 372 hectares of natural forest in the very first year feels heavy on the woodland. Shouldn't we keep year-one forest cutting under 250 hectares? What's the tradeoff the model is making there?
expected_pyomo: model.forest_cut_y01_tight = Constraint(expr=sum(model.w[1, i] for i in model.i) <= 250)

query: The village is committing an awful lot of land to tobacco over the years. Couldn't we just cap total tobacco at 5500 hectares across the horizon and lean more on food crops? What's the model getting out of all that tobacco?
expected_pyomo: model.tobacco_total_tight = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[t, 'tobacco'] for t in model.t) <= 5500)

query: Shouldn't the village be planting more of its own woodlot early instead of leaning on the natural forest? Why not require at least 700 hectares of managed timber across the first five years? What's the model seeing that makes it hold back on planting?
